# I Blame This 1769 Color Wheel for Centuries of Bad Color Theory!

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube)
**Date:** 2025-01-14
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bAIWcrBN6CY
**Duration:** 2:08
**Views:** 2,502

## Description

How Moses Harris's 1769 color wheel became the template for centuries of flawed RYB color theory — and why the wheel itself couldn't have been painted using its own theory.

## Transcript

This color wheel is lying. Sure it's pretty — you've probably seen it around, you can even buy it as a poster. But let's talk about it.

Around 1769, Moses Harris published this hand-colored etching in a small booklet titled "The Natural System of Colors." Is it the first artist's color wheel? No — he did beat the German Ignaz Schiffermüller by a few years, but way back in 1708, an unknown French author (possibly Claude Boutet) published this color wheel. And we can't go any further back than that because 1704 was when Newton's Opticks was published — this diagram is considered to be the first true color wheel.

Harris took inspiration from Newton's circle and probably also Brook Taylor's 1719 description of how you could use Newton's circle as an artist to predict the mixture of paints — even though it's really a diagram of light mixture, not paint mixture. And indeed Harris called his diagram a "prismatic," and the triangles at the center represent three translucent shards of colored glass illustrating subtractive mixing — or more properly, illustrating the fact that Harris didn't quite understand the difference between additive and subtractive mixing.

**Harris seems to be the first person to place the so-called primaries — red, yellow, and blue — equidistant around the circle, 120° apart.** Newton didn't. Boutet didn't. Schiffermüller didn't. Those circles all base their hues more or less on how they are spaced in a prismatic spectrum.

Furthermore, Harris's exemplar of red is vermilion, his yellow is King's yellow (a refined kind of orpiment), his blue is ultramarine. These primaries will of course give a decent orange, a middling green, and a yucky purple.

And in this way, Harris's mixing theory is actually a step back from Boutet (or whoever's) mixing theory in 1708, which acknowledges the need for **two primary reds** in paint mixing — a crimson red and a fire red.

And in fact, as a friend pointed out to me, Harris's hand-colored wheel must include more than three pigments — there's absolutely no way he could have gotten this vivid red-violet by mixing his vermilion and ultramarine blue. Probably carmine lake.

## Key Facts

- **Moses Harris (1769)** — "The Natural System of Colors"; first to place RYB equally spaced at 120° apart
- **Earlier wheels:** Boutet/unknown (1708), Newton (1704 — the first true color wheel), Brook Taylor (1719)
- **Harris's pigments:** vermilion (red), King's yellow/orpiment (yellow), ultramarine (blue)
- **The self-contradiction:** Harris's own wheel couldn't be painted with only his three primaries — needed carmine lake for the vivid red-violet
- **Boutet (1708) was smarter:** acknowledged need for TWO reds (crimson + fire red)
- **Harris confused additive and subtractive** — called his diagram "prismatic" but used paint mixing
- **Newton, Boutet, Schiffermüller** all spaced hues based on the actual prismatic spectrum — Harris imposed arbitrary equal 120° spacing
- **Legacy:** 250+ years of teachers using this erroneous equally-spaced RYB wheel

## Timeline of Color Wheels

| Year | Author         | Key Feature                                                   |
| ---- | -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 1704 | Newton         | First true color circle (in Opticks) — light, not paint       |
| 1708 | Boutet (?)     | First artist color wheel; two reds (crimson + fire)           |
| 1719 | Brook Taylor   | Proposed using Newton's circle for paint mixing               |
| 1769 | Moses Harris   | First RYB at 120° equal spacing — the template for bad theory |
| 1772 | Schiffermüller | Similar to Harris but independently developed                 |
